scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isaeus was an Athenian orator whose speeches exclusively concern Athenian inheritance and civic law.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to a specific Athenian law regarding inheritance through male vs. female lines.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isaeus describes Athenian social and legal customs regarding dowries and citizen status.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly mentions the archonship of Eucleides, a turning point for Athenian citizenship rules.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This describes the procedure of the 'diapsephisis' (revision of citizen rolls) occurring specifically in the Athenian demes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The penalty of enslavement for failed citizenship claims was a draconian feature of 4th-century Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Date reflects the general revision of the citizen registers in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Confiscation of property (demiosion) was a standard Athenian penalty for those convicted of usurping citizen rights.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The double-descent requirement (Pericles' citizenship law) was the fundamental status rule in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule is argued within the context of the 346 BCE citizen list revision.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The enrollment in the phratry (ward) was an Athenian institution for validating legitimate male descent.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Date corresponds to the legal proceedings regarding the status of Euphiletus.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: While the festival was common to Greeks, the play specifically satirizes the female-only assembly of the Athenian Thesmophoria.
